title: Improvements in superconducting switches
abstract: There is provided an electrical switch comprising a length of superconducting material. In some forms the electrical switch is configured to be controlled between a low-resistance state and a higher-resistance state by the selective application of one or more switching mechanisms. In the higher-resistance state, current flowing through the length of superconducting material may approach the critical current of the length of superconducting material, may be substantially equal to the critical current or may be greater than the critical current. In some forms, the length of superconducting material is a length of high temperature superconducting material. The switching mechanisms may comprise: heating the length of superconducting material; applying a magnetic field to the length of superconducting material; applying a time-varying magnetic field to create dynamic resistance and/or heat; and/or applying a time-varying magnetic field to a loop of superconducting material to create a screening current around the loop.
claims:
Claims 49 Claims An electrical switch comprising: a length of superconducting material configured to carry a transport current, wherein the length of superconducting material has a critical current and a critical temperature; a magnetic field generator configured to apply a magnetic field to the length of superconducting material; and a heating assembly for heating the length of superconducting material, wherein the magnetic field generator and the heating assembly are configured to be selectively controlled independently or in combination to switch the length of superconducting material between a low-resistance state and a higher resistance state, wherein, in the low-resistance state, a magnitude of the magnetic field is relatively low and a temperature of the length of superconducting material is substantially less than the critical temperature such that the transport current is substantially less than the critical current, wherein, in a first configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the magnitude of the magnetic field is relatively high to reduce the critical current; wherein, in a second configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the heating assembly heats the length of superconducting material to reduce the critical current, wherein, in a third configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the magnitude of the magnetic field is relatively high and the heating assembly heats the length of superconducting material to reduce the critical current, and wherein, in each of the configurations of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the critical current is reduced such that the transport current approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the length of superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 1, wherein the magnetic field generator and the heating assembly are configured to be selectively activated and de-activated independently or in combination to switch the electrical switch between the low-resistance state and the higher resistance state. 

 50 An electrical switch as claimed in claim 2, wherein the heating assembly is configured to remain activated and the length of superconducting material is switched between the low-resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the magnetic field generator. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-3, wherein the magnetic field applied by the magnetic field generator is a constant magnetic field. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-4, wherein the heating assembly comprises a resistive heating element positioned in thermal contact with the length of superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-5, wherein the superconducting material is a high-temperature superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-6, wherein the magnetic field generator is a first magnetic field generator and the magnetic field is a first magnetic field, and wherein the electrical switch comprises a second magnetic field generator configured to apply a second, time-varying, magnetic field to the length of superconducting material, wherein the second magnetic field generator is configured to be selectively controlled to switch the length of superconducting material between the low-resistance state and the higher resistance state. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 7, wherein the superconducting material is a tape having two opposed faces, and wherein the second magnetic field generator is configured to apply the second magnetic field in a direction substantially perpendicular to the two opposed faces. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 7 or 8, wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are the same magnetic field generator, wherein the magnitude of the magnetic field applied by the same magnetic field generator varies in time with a DC bias. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-9, wherein: the length of superconducting material comprises a loop of superconducting material configured to carry the transport current between a first terminal and a second terminal, 

 51 wherein the loop comprises a first branch and a second branch, the first and second branches being electrically connected in parallel between the first terminal and the second terminal, and wherein the loop has an axis which is substantially normal to the plane of the loop, and the electrical switch further comprises: a third, time-varying, magnetic field generator configured to apply a third, timevarying, magnetic field through the loop with the direction of the third magnetic field through the loop being generally parallel to, or having a component which is generally parallel to, the axis of the loop, wherein, in the low-resistance state, the third magnetic field generator does not apply the third magnetic field through the loop and the transport current flows through the loop between the two terminals, and wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the third magnetic field generator applies the third magnetic field through the loop, inducing a screening current in the loop such that a total current in one or more of the first branch and the second branch approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 1-10, wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the length of superconducting material is in a superconducting state. An electrical switch comprising: a length of superconducting material configured to carry a transport current, wherein the length of superconducting material has a critical current and a critical temperature; a first magnetic field generator configured to apply a first, constant, magnetic field to the length of superconducting material; and a second magnetic field generator configured to apply a second, time-varying, magnetic field to the length of superconducting material, wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are configured to be selectively controlled independently or in combination to switch the length of superconducting material between a low-resistance state and a higher resistance state, 

 52 wherein, in the low-resistance state, magnitudes of the first magnetic field and the second magnetic field are relatively low and the transport current is substantially less than the critical current, wherein, in a first configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the magnitude of the first magnetic field is relatively high to reduce the critical current such that the transport current approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the length of superconducting material, wherein, in a second configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the second magnetic field creates dynamic resistance in the length of superconducting material, and wherein, in a third configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the magnitude of the first magnetic field is relatively high to reduce the critical current such that the transport current approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the length of superconducting material, and the second magnetic field creates dynamic resistance in the length of superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 12, wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are configured to be selectively activated and de-activated independently or in combination to switch the length of superconducting material between the low-resistance state and the higher resistance state. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 13, wherein the first magnetic field generator is configured to remain activated and the length of superconducting material is switched between the low-resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the second magnetic field generator. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 13, wherein the second magnetic field generator is configured to remain activated and the length of superconducting material is switched between the low-resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the first magnetic field generator. 

An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-15, wherein the superconducting material is a tape having two opposed faces, and wherein the second magnetic field generator is configured to apply the second magnetic field in a direction substantially perpendicular to the two opposed faces. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-16, wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are the same magnetic field generator, wherein the magnitude of the magnetic field applied by the same magnetic field generator varies in time with a DC bias. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-17, wherein the superconducting material is a high-temperature superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-18, wherein the electrical switch further comprises a heating assembly for heating the length of superconducting material, wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the heating assembly heats the length of superconducting material to reduce the critical current such that the transport current approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the length of superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 19, wherein the heating assembly comprises a resistive heating element positioned in thermal contact with the length of superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-20, wherein: the length of superconducting material comprises a loop of superconducting material configured to carry the transport current between a first terminal and a second terminal, wherein the loop comprises a first branch and a second branch, the first and second branches being electrically connected in parallel between the first terminal and the second terminal, and wherein the loop has an axis which is substantially normal to the plane of the loop, and the electrical switch further comprises: a third, time-varying, magnetic field generator configured to apply a third, timevarying, magnetic field through the loop with the direction of the third magnetic field 

 through the loop being generally parallel to, or having a component which is generally parallel to, the axis of the loop, wherein, in the low-resistance state, the third magnetic field generator does not apply the third magnetic field through the loop and the transport current flows through the loop between the two terminals, and wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the third magnetic field generator applies the third magnetic field through the loop, inducing a screening current in the loop such that a total current in one or more of the first branch and the second branch approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the superconducting material. 22. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 12-21, wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the length of superconducting material is in a superconducting state. 23. An electrical switch comprising: a loop of superconducting material configured to carry a transport current between a first terminal and a second terminal, wherein the loop of superconducting material has a critical current and a critical temperature, and wherein the loop comprises a first branch and a second branch, the first and second branches being electrically connected in parallel between the first terminal and the second terminal, and wherein the loop has an axis which is substantially normal to the plane of the loop; a time-varying magnetic field generator configured to apply a time-varying magnetic field through the loop with the direction of the magnetic field through the loop being generally parallel to, or having a component which is generally parallel to, the axis of the loop; and a heating assembly for heating the loop of superconducting material, wherein the time-varying magnetic field generator and the heating assembly are configured to be selectively controlled independently or in combination to switch the loop of superconducting material between a low-resistance state and a higher-resistance state, wherein, in the low-resistance state: the time-varying magnetic field generator does not apply the time-varying magnetic field through the loop and the transport current flows through the loop 

 55 between the two terminals, the transport current being substantially less than the critical current; and a temperature of the loop is substantially less than the critical temperature, wherein, in a first configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the time-varying magnetic field generator applies the time-varying magnetic field through the loop, inducing a screening current in the loop, wherein, in a second configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the heating assembly heats the loop to reduce the critical current, wherein, in a third configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the time-varying magnetic field generator applies the time-varying magnetic field through the loop, inducing a screening current in the loop, and the heating assembly heats the loop to reduce the critical current, and wherein, in each of the configurations of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, a total current in one or more of the first branch and the second branch approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 23, wherein the time-varying magnetic field generator and the heating assembly are configured to be selectively activated and de-activated independently or in combination to switch the loop of superconducting material between the low-resistance state and the higher resistance state. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 24, wherein the heating assembly is configured to remain activated and the loop of superconducting material is switched between the low- resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the time-varying magnetic field generator. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-25, wherein the heating assembly comprises a resistive heating element positioned in thermal contact with the loop of superconducting material. 

 56 27. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-26, wherein the first branch comprises one or more coils of superconducting material. 28. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-27, wherein the second branch comprises one or more coils of superconducting material. 29. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 28 when dependent on claim 27, wherein the coils of the first branch are wound around the same axis as the coils of the second branch. 30. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 29 or claim 28 when dependent on claim 27, wherein the coils of the first branch are wound in a first rotation direction and the coils of the second branch are wound in a second rotation direction, wherein the first rotation direction is different to the second rotation direction. 31. An electrical switch as claimed in any one claims 23-30, wherein the time-varying magnetic field generator is magnetically coupled to the loop of superconducting material by a core. 32. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-31, wherein the superconducting material is a high-temperature superconducting material. 33. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-32, wherein the electrical switch further comprises a second magnetic field generator configured to apply a second magnetic field to the loop of superconducting material, wherein, in the low-resistance state, a magnitude of the second magnetic field is relatively low, and wherein, in the high-resistance state, the magnitude of the second magnetic field is relatively high to reduce the critical current. 34. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 23-33, wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the loop of superconducting material is in a superconducting state. 35. An electrical switch comprising: a loop of superconducting material configured to carry a transport current between a first terminal and a second terminal, wherein the loop of superconducting material has a critical 

 57 current and a critical temperature, and wherein the loop comprises a first branch and a second branch, the first and second branches being electrically connected in parallel between the first terminal and the second terminal, and wherein the loop has an axis which is substantially normal to the plane of the loop; a first magnetic field generator configured to apply a time-varying magnetic field through the loop with the direction of the magnetic field through the loop being generally parallel to, or having a component which is generally parallel to, the axis of the loop; and a second magnetic field generator configured to apply a second magnetic field to the length of superconducting material; wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are configured to be selectively controlled independently or in combination to switch the loop of superconducting material between a low-resistance state and a higher-resistance state, wherein, in the low-resistance state, the first magnetic field generator does not apply the time-varying magnetic field through the loop and the transport current flows through the loop between the two terminals and a magnitude of the second magnetic field is relatively low such that the transport current is substantially less than the critical current, wherein, in a first configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state the first magnetic field generator applies the time-varying magnetic field through the loop, inducing a screening current in the loop, wherein, in a second configuration of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, the magnitude of the second magnetic field is relatively high to reduce the critical current, and wherein, in each of the configurations of the electrical switch in the higher-resistance state, a total current in one or more of the first branch and the second branch approaches the critical current, is substantially equal to the critical current or is greater than the critical current of the superconducting material. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 35, wherein the first magnetic field generator and the second magnetic field generator are configured to be selectively activated and de-activated independently or in combination to switch the loop of superconducting material between the low-resistance state and the higher resistance state. 

 58 37. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 36, wherein the first magnetic field generator is configured to remain activated and the loop of superconducting material is switched between the low-resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the second magnetic field generator. 38. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 36, wherein the second magnetic field generator is configured to remain activated and the loop of superconducting material is switched between the low-resistance state and the higher-resistance state by selective de-activation and activation of the first magnetic field generator. 39. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-38, wherein the first branch comprises one or more coils of superconducting material. 40. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-39, wherein the second branch comprises one or more coils of superconducting material. 41. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 40 when dependent on claim 39, wherein the coils of the first branch are wound around the same axis as the coils of the second branch. 42. An electrical switch as claimed in claim 41 or claim 40 when dependent on claim 39, wherein the coils of the first branch are wound in a first rotation direction and the coils of the second branch are wound in a second rotation direction, wherein the first rotation direction is different to the second rotation direction. 43. An electrical switch as claimed in any one claims 35-42, wherein the first magnetic field generator is magnetically coupled to the loop of superconducting material by a core. 44. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-43, wherein the second magnetic field applied by the second magnetic field generator is a constant magnetic field. 45. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-44, wherein the superconducting material is a high-temperature superconducting material. 

 59 An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-45, wherein the electrical switch further comprises a heating assembly for heating the loop of superconducting material, wherein, in the low-resistance state, a temperature of the loop is substantially less than the critical temperature, and wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the heating assembly heats the loop to reduce the critical current. An electrical switch as claimed in any one of claims 35-46, wherein, in the higher-resistance state, the loop of superconducting material is in a superconducting state.
